ns0:r.ns0:t: The Agent system is composed of four independent layers that communicate through well-defined interfaces. Each layer has a single responsibility, enabling independent scaling, replacement, and testing. A cloud adapter interface is reserved in every layer to allow future migration from local/self-hosted components to managed cloud services without business logic changes.
Task planning, tool routing, context compression, token budget
Tool calls, sub-agents, concurrency, retry logic, sandboxing
Short-term memory, long-term memory, vector retrieval, state
Tenant isolation, RBAC, audit log, rate limit, secrets

No layer should call another layer's internal logic directly. All cross-layer communication passes through the layer's public interface (function call, HTTP endpoint, or message queue). This guarantees tenant isolation and testability.

The orchestration layer is the 'brain' of the agent. It receives user intent, decomposes it into sub-tasks, decides which tools to call, and enforces the token budget. It does NOT execute tools directly — that is the execution layer's job.
2.1 Planning Model: ReAct Pattern (Planned; not implemented in this repository)

Design note: ReAct (Reasoning + Acting) is a recommended pattern. This repository currently uses a pipeline-based orchestrator (intent complexity dispatch integrate) rather than a generic ReAct loop. A ReAct loop may be added later.
thought = llm(context) # what to do next
The context manager monitors token usage on every iteration and applies compression strategies in priority order:
Level 1 — Tool result truncation: trim long tool outputs to a max length (e.g. 2,000 tokens).
Level 2 — History summarization: when context exceeds 60% of budget, call a cheap model to summarize older turns and replace them.
Level 3 — Working memory offload: serialize intermediate state to episodic storage and load only the current step's state into context.
Level 4 — Hard stop: if budget would be exceeded and no compression is possible, return a partial result with a BUDGET_EXCEEDED flag.
Never include all tool schemas in the system prompt. Use a two-stage injection:
Stage 1 (intent classification): send user message to a cheap model (Haiku) with a minimal prompt to classify intent into one of N task categories.
Stage 2 (tool whitelist): load only the tool schemas relevant to that task category. A task category 'code' might whitelist [run_code, read_file, write_file] while excluding [web_search, send_email].
Token cost is the primary operational cost driver for production agent systems. The following strategies are listed in order of implementation priority (highest ROI first):
Cache fixed prefix (system prompt, tool schemas)
Retrieve top-k chunks instead of full doc
Only include relevant tool schemas per task
Small model for triage, large for reasoning

Implement a BudgetController class that wraps every LLM call. It tracks cumulative token usage per session, enforces per-call max_tokens, and triggers the compression pipeline when the session budget reaches 80%. This must be a cross-cutting concern applied at the orchestration layer, not inside individual tool handlers.

Anthropic's cache_control parameter marks content blocks as cacheable. The cache persists for 5 minutes (ephemeral) and reduces input token cost by approximately 90% on cache hits. Structure your system prompt with stable content first:

Agent memory is modeled as four distinct types, each with a different backend optimized for its access pattern and lifetime. All backends are accessed through a single MemoryAdapter interface so that the implementation can be swapped without changing orchestration logic.
Every memory backend must implement this interface. Cloud backends (Pinecone, Supabase) and local backends (ChromaDB, SQLite) are interchangeable at configuration time:
Lightweight / MVP: Redis (working mem) + SQLite + Mem0 local (episodic) + pgvector (semantic) + local filesystem (procedural)
Production: Upstash Redis + PostgreSQL + Mem0 cloud (episodic) + Pinecone (semantic) + S3 + Temporal.io (procedural)
Single-DB option: Supabase (PostgreSQL + pgvector + Auth + Storage) covers episodic + semantic + auth in one managed service.

Multi-tenant agent systems have a fundamentally different threat model from single-user applications. An agent can be manipulated through its inputs (prompt injection), its tool outputs (malicious tool responses), or through cross-tenant data leakage. Each must be mitigated at the layer where it originates.
Pinecone namespaces, PostgreSQL RLS, Redis key prefix per tenant
Code / tool calls run in MicroVM or container sandbox
Per-tenant token quota + request rate cap
API keys / credentials never in context or logs
Any tool that executes code, runs shell commands, or makes outbound network requests MUST run in an isolated environment. Minimum requirements for the sandbox:
No access to host filesystem outside of /tmp (ephemeral)
No access to the host network except explicitly whitelisted domains
CPU and memory hard limits (e.g. 0.5 CPU, 512 MB RAM)
No ability to escalate privileges or access other tenant namespaces
Prompt injection occurs when a tool's output contains instructions that cause the LLM to deviate from the user's intent. Mitigations:
Wrap all tool results in a fixed XML envelope: <tool_result>...</tool_result>. Instruct the model in the system prompt that instructions inside this envelope are data, not commands.
Validate tool output schema before inserting into context. Reject any tool result that contains tokens resembling system-level instructions.
Run a lightweight guard model on each tool result to classify it as SAFE or SUSPICIOUS before it enters the main context.

Each agent instance is configured by a JSON/YAML object that is validated at startup. The schema includes reserved fields for future cloud and telemetry integration. All fields with null defaults are no-ops until explicitly set.
LLM model identifier; controls cost/capability tier
Max reasoning steps per task; prevents infinite loops
Max tokens per session across all calls
Whitelist of tool names to inject for this agent instance
none | session | long — controls which memory layers activate
none | e2b | docker — execution isolation for tool calls
Reserved: endpoint for OpenTelemetry trace export

The cloud_adapter field activates a provider-specific implementation of the storage and execution interfaces. All providers implement the same abstract base classes, so the orchestration layer requires no changes when switching providers. To add a new cloud provider:
Implement StorageAdapter, ExecutionAdapter, and SecretsAdapter for the target provider.
Register the implementation under the provider key (e.g. 'aws', 'gcp').
Set cloud_adapter in the agent config. The DI container resolves the correct implementation at runtime.

When deploying to cloud, the telemetry_url field should point to an OpenTelemetry-compatible collector endpoint. All LLM calls, tool calls, token counts, and latency metrics are automatically emitted as spans. No code changes are required — the instrumentation is in the MemoryAdapter and ExecutionAdapter base classes.
Build in this sequence to validate the architecture incrementally:

Step 1 Orchestration only: if adopting ReAct, implement the ReAct loop with a single LLM, no tools, no memory. Validate the token budget controller. (Planned; current repository uses pipeline orchestration.)
Step 2 — Tool execution: add tool calling with a local Docker sandbox. Implement prompt caching.
Step 3 — Working memory: add Redis-backed session state. Implement context compression.
Step 4 — Semantic memory: add pgvector for document retrieval. Replace static system prompt knowledge with RAG.
Step 5 — Multi-tenancy: add PostgreSQL RLS, per-tenant Redis key namespacing, and OPA policy enforcement.
Step 6 — Observability: integrate Langfuse or LangSmith. Add structured audit logs.
Step 7 — Cloud migration: swap local adapters for managed cloud services via the cloud_adapter config field.
The following decisions require product input before implementation:
Episodic memory TTL policy: how long should per-user conversation history be retained? (regulatory, GDPR, cost implications)
Cross-tenant semantic search: should tenants ever share a vector namespace (e.g. shared knowledge base)? Requires explicit access control design.
Sub-agent trust model: when a parent agent spawns a child agent, does the child inherit the parent's capability token or receive a restricted subset?
Prompt injection response: when a guard model flags a tool result as SUSPICIOUS, should the system (a) skip the result, (b) quarantine the session, or (c) alert and continue?
Model failover: if the primary model provider is unavailable, is there an approved fallback provider? This affects the cloud_adapter abstraction.
